text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to remove adhesions from a previous caesarean section, preparing the surgical field for the laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The video shows a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids, including large anterior and posterior fibroids. The sigmoid colon is visible adjacent to the uterus.

The surgeon addresses adhesions between the ovary and surrounding structures, including the ovarian fossa and uterus. The fallopian tube is visible, and slight mobilization of the sigmoid colon provides better exposure of the ureter and adnexal structures.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and the infundibulopelvic ligament, noting the ureter's upward course. The procedure will include bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) as part of the hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses a ligature in the right hand and a bipolar grasper in the left hand to cut the round ligament and dissect the fallopian tube. The infundibulopelvic ligament is separated, and the anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament are opened. The uterus is given contralateral traction, and the round ligament on the left side is being separated.

The surgeon identifies the ovary, which appears affected by endometriosis, and the infundibulopelvic ligament. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is visible. The uterus is pushed towards the upper abdomen using a uterine manipulator. The ovary, adhered to the ovarian fossa, is being separated.

The surgeon pushes the uterus further into the abdominal cavity, preparing for additional dissection. The round ligament and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament are targeted for separation, with the uterus being retroverted to facilitate access.

The surgeon carefully separates the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, maintaining a medial approach to avoid lateral deviation. The fallopian tube and ovary are being separated from the uterus to improve visibility and prevent interference. Coagulation is applied to control any bleeding that occurs during the separation.

The surgeon separates the uterovesical fold of the peritoneum on the opposite side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The bladder is carefully dissected and moved upward. A fibroid is visible, obstructing the view, but a third-digit telescope is used to enhance visualization.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder using a harmonic scalpel, encountering difficulty due to a fibroid obstructing the view. The fibroid causes challenges in achieving a clear separation plane, requiring slow and meticulous dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from surrounding tissues to prevent injury to the ureter, particularly in cases with multiple fibroids and adhesions from previous surgeries. The bladder is visibly elevated, revealing the vaginal fascia.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect further pedicles, revealing the shiny surface of the bladder pillars. The uterine artery becomes visible, and the ligature is applied to the lower pedicles, maintaining proximity to the uterus.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate the uterine artery, with some bleeding visible as collected blood is released. The dissection progresses to the opposite side, with the right side dissection primarily performed from the left side, providing a clear surgical plane. The bladder has been separated from the left side using an ipsilateral port, and the telescope is being cleaned.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the uterine artery, which is being skeletonized. The uterine artery is coagulated using an electrosurgical device.

The surgeon addresses adhesions on the posterior side due to a large fibroid, using the ligature from the same side instead of the opposite direction. The uterine is held from the right side, making it perpendicular. The bladder is fully separated, and a full circle colpotomizer is pushed into place, with its ring visibly encircling the vaginal cuff.

The surgeon addresses posterior adhesions using a harmonic device, cleaning its tip for optimal function. The ligature is applied to further veins and the macundrat, separating them from surrounding tissue. The artery has been previously separated.
The surgeon begins colpotomy, cutting the vaginal tissue over the white tip of the colpotomizer. Bleeding is observed at the angles of the vagina, particularly posteriorly due to venous involvement.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the uterine artery before applying lateral coagulation using a harmonic or hook device, ensuring safer coagulation in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device, taking care to avoid cutting the tip of the colpotomizer. The view shifts to the posterior, revealing adhesions around the utero sacral ligament on either side. The cervical part of the utero sacral ligament is visibly cut.

The assistant pushes the colpotomizer into position for colpotomy, facilitating the procedure from the same side. The colpotomy on the right side is completed, and the uterus is prepared for removal. The surgeon applies a speculum for vaginal bisection and begins suturing.

The surgeon uses a number one vicryl suture to perform intracorporeal suturing with a seizure knot technique. The suture is wrapped in a C shape with a double wrap, ensuring the vaginal epithelium is included. The bladder is carefully separated, and continuous suturing is performed with locking stitches.

The surgeon performs wall closure after a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, ensuring careful dissection and precise suturing. The lateral angles of the uterus are secured, and the suture is tightened by holding the suture rather than the needle.
The surgeon performs irrigation and suction to clean the surgical area, checking for any bleeding and thoroughly examining the pelvis. The ten millimeter ports are closed following the procedure.
